ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic hot pot class (FOODON_03315595); no specific Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the primary protein anchor for this mixed hot pot dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot as a composite dish. Cooked chicken meat code A0CKM used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional composite hot pot dishes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 individual bowl (approximately 500g from shared hot pot)
serving.natural_language_portion: One individual serving drawn from a shared hot pot is approximately 500g, comprising broth, kampung chicken pieces, fresh vegetables, and a portion of vermicelli noodles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broiler or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed (FDC ID 331960) and FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in Southeast Asia — vegetable and broth components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated composites derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 331960 (chicken, broiler or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed) as the primary protein anchor, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table data for vegetable and broth components (cabbage, morning glory, mushrooms, lemongrass, ginger). The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against these approved sources and found broadly consistent with a mixed chicken-vegetable hot pot at the stated dilution ratio. Values retained with medium confidence and documented as estimated composites. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source; it is cited only as the menu listing origin in the input record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate for mixed chicken-vegetable broth hot pot via University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot (Lẩu gà ớt hiểm) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken-vegetable soup and hot pot entries available in the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com). The dish is predominantly protein and vegetable with a modest noodle component; broth dilution, high protein content (10g/100g), and low sugar (0.67g/100g) all attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 500g individual serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 26.65) / 100 = 10.66, rounded to 10.7. The peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 tables (Diabetes Care) are cited as the methodological basis for GI category classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300mg/100g; 1,500mg per 500g individual serving approaches the 1,500–2,000mg daily limit for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the broth, limit broth consumption to reduce sodium load, and choose plain vermicelli over instant noodles to avoid additional sodium; the estimated potassium content (~210mg/100g from vegetables and chicken) provides partial offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium burden at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.33g/100g), low sugar (0.67g/100g), high protein (10g/100g), and estimated low GI (~40) collectively attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish with low glycaemic concern provided they choose plain vermicelli or glass noodles over instant noodles, moderate total noodle portion to limit refined carbohydrate load, and prioritise mushrooms and leafy vegetables which further support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.67g/100g; kampung (free-range) chicken has a leaner fat profile than commercial broiler chicken; no deep-frying involved
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease can include this dish as a heart-friendly option given its low saturated fat, lean protein source, and vegetable-rich composition, but should limit broth intake to manage sodium and request reduced fish sauce to keep total sodium within cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (ginger, garlic, lemongrass) and beta-glucan-rich mushrooms; low saturated fat; no deep-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of this dish, but those with nightshade sensitivity should reduce or omit bird's eye chili (ớt hiểm), and all RA patients should request reduced fish sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kampung Hot Pot (Vietnamese: Lẩu gà ớt hiểm) is a traditional Vietnamese communal hot pot dish featuring kampung chicken — a free-range breed prized for its leaner, firmer meat and more pronounced flavour compared to commercial broiler chicken. The dish is prepared by simmering kampung chicken pieces in a spicy, aromatic broth built from bird's eye chili (ớt hiểm), lemongrass, ginger, garlic, and fish sauce. The broth is characteristically clear to lightly golden, intensely fragrant, and moderately to strongly spicy. Diners cook fresh vegetables — typically cabbage, morning glory (rau muống), and mushrooms — directly in the simmering broth at the table, along with vermicelli or glass noodles. The dish is served communally, with the hot pot placed at the centre of the table. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 120 kcal, 10g protein, 6g fat (saturated fat 1.67g), 5.33g carbohydrate, 0.67g sugar, 1g dietary fibre, and 300mg sodium. The high protein content and low carbohydrate load result in a low estimated glycaemic index (~40) and low glycaemic load per serving (~10.7 for a 500g individual portion). The dish is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low sugar and moderate carbohydrate content, and for cardiovascular health due to its lean protein source and low saturated fat. Sodium at 300mg/100g warrants caution for hypertensive individuals, particularly from concentrated broth consumption. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, garlic, lemongrass) and beta-glucan-rich mushrooms make this dish of interest for inflammatory conditions. It is halal-suitable and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot as a composite dish. Nutrient values from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 331960, chicken stewed meat only) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table data for vegetable and broth components, and found broadly consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 331960 (chicken, broiler or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed) as the primary protein anchor, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table data for vegetable and broth components. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient evidence source; it is the menu listing origin of the input record only. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Vietnamese kampung chicken hot pot as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the composite dish level as of 2026. 3. potassium_mg set to 210.0 (estimated): derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable and chicken component data; not directly measured for this composite dish. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese hot pot dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the composite dish level as of 2026. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on mixed chicken-vegetable soup and hot pot entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com); no direct GI measurement for Lẩu gà ớt hiểm has been published in peer-reviewed literature. 6. Halal status assessed as likely true given chicken and vegetable composition; fish sauce brand-level halal certification should be verified for specific restaurant preparations. 7. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore (GrabFood listing origin); region_of_origin is correctly set to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.03g/100g is retained from the input record as a trace value consistent with minor ruminant trans-fat contribution from chicken fat; this is not industrially produced trans fat.
